steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Mulder and Spellman concluded that invasives are advantaged by delays in subfreezing temperature onset. To support this, a finding must show that when freezing is delayed, the invasive species (P. aviculare) benefits more than the native (V. edule) — a clear differential advantage for the invasive.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — both extend leaf production later, but the extension is much greater for P. aviculare: when freezing is delayed, the invasive gains significantly more growing time than the native. This is a direct differential advantage for the invasive. ✓
  • Choice B — neither species shows any interannual variation in leaf cessation: if neither responds to temperature delays, neither is advantaged. This contradicts the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — P. aviculare stops producing leaves sooner than V. edule when freezing is late: the invasive ends its season earlier, meaning the native benefits more — reverses the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice D — both produce more leaves, but late-freezing years also had early spring onset: a confounding variable (early spring) makes it impossible to attribute any advantage specifically to late subfreezing onset. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
